network.overall_strategy: Seven experimentally demonstrated de novo routes to glycine converge from three prebiotic environments: (1) Hydrothermal iron-catalyzed reductive amination of glyoxylate, peracetic acid amination, and thermal decomposition of higher amino acids (serine, threonine, asparagine); (2) Surface cyanosulfidic Strecker synthesis via aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis and UV photoredox generation of glyoxylate; (3) Biochemical peptide assembly from accumulated glycine. Glyoxylate serves as the principal hub molecule, fed by both hydrothermal retro-aldol cleavage of isocitrate (Muchowska et al., 2019) and surface UV photoredox dehydration (Liu et al., 2021). Formaldehyde and ammonia are secondary hubs connecting Strecker and retro-aldol chemistries. Glycolic acid links the C2 metabolic network via reduction/Cannizzaro/photoredox side routes. The network demonstrates glycine as a thermodynamic attractor in the prebiotic amino acid landscape.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: UV-irradiated surface pool, cyanosulfidic conditions with H2S present

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde reacts with ammonia to form an imine (methyleneimine, CH2=NH), which is attacked by cyanide (HCN) in a nucleophilic addition to yield aminoacetonitrile (H2N-CH2-CN). This is the classic Strecker aminonitrile synthesis applied to the simplest aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy, and Sutherland (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202) demonstrated that aminoacetonitrile is the Strecker intermediate formed from formaldehyde, HCN, and NH3 under cyanosulfidic conditions. This is part of the unified cyanosulfidic protometabolism that produces precursors to RNA, proteins, and lipids from simple feedstocks. Sulfide minerals (stibnite) and clay surfaces (montmorillonite) concentrate reactants and catalyze the condensation in surface pools.

reactions.name: Aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis to glycine

conditions.other: Wet-dry cycling promotes hydrolysis; may proceed through aminoacetamide intermediate

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of water to the nitrile group of aminoacetonitrile forms 2-aminoacetamide (H2N-CH2-CONH2) as an intermediate, which further hydrolyzes to glycine (H2N-CH2-COOH) with release of ammonia (NH3). This is a two-step hydrolysis: nitrile → amide → carboxylic acid.
reactions.reasoning: Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy, and Sutherland (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202) showed that aminoacetonitrile, the Strecker aminonitrile intermediate, hydrolyzes to glycine under aqueous conditions. Montmorillonite clay concentrates organic molecules on its surface and can catalyze hydrolysis reactions in evaporitic surface pools with wet-dry cycling.

reactions.name: Iron-catalyzed retro-aldol cleavage of isocitrate to glyoxylate

conditions.other: 70°C, mildly alkaline, analogous to biological isocitrate lyase

reactions.mechanism: Fe2+ acts as a Lewis acid to coordinate the hydroxyl and carboxylate groups of isocitrate, polarizing the C-C bond and facilitating retro-aldol cleavage. The C6 tricarboxylic acid is split into glyoxylate (C2) and succinate (C4). This is the prebiotic analog of the isocitrate lyase reaction.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska, Varma, and Moran (2019, Nature, DOI: 10.1038/s41586-019-1151-1) demonstrated Fe2+-catalyzed retro-aldol cleavage of isocitrate at 70°C in water, producing glyoxylate and succinate. This reaction is part of an interconnected iron-driven reaction network that interconverts universal metabolic precursors. Mackinawite (FeS) provides a mineral surface and additional Fe species in hydrothermal settings.

reactions.name: UV photoredox dehydration of 2,2-dihydroxyacetic acid to glyoxylate

conditions.other: UV-irradiated shallow pool, no ozone shielding on early Earth

reactions.mechanism: UV photons drive dehydration of the gem-diol form (2,2-dihydroxyacetic acid) to regenerate the aldehyde functional group, producing glyoxylate. TiO2 (anatase) acts as a semiconductor photocatalyst that absorbs UV and generates reactive radical intermediates facilitating the dehydration.
reactions.reasoning: Liu, Wu, Kufner, Sasselov, Fischer, and Sutherland (2021, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/s41557-021-00789-w) demonstrated UV-driven dehydration of 2,2-dihydroxyacetic acid to glyoxylate under prebiotic UV conditions. This provides a photochemical surface-environment route to glyoxylate, the key C2 alpha-keto acid hub molecule, complementing the hydrothermal iron-catalyzed route from isocitrate.

reactions.name: Iron-catalyzed reductive amination of glyoxylate to glycine

conditions.other: 70°C, aqueous, alkaline hydrothermal vent mixing zone; hydroxylamine as nitrogen donor

reactions.mechanism: Iron-promoted reductive amination: hydroxylamine (NH2OH) acts as the nitrogen donor, condensing with the aldehyde group of glyoxylate to form an oxime intermediate. Fe0 provides reducing equivalents to reduce the oxime/imine to the amine, converting the alpha-keto acid (glyoxylate) to the corresponding alpha-amino acid (glycine). Two equivalents of water are released.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska, Varma, and Moran (2019, Nature, DOI: 10.1038/s41586-019-1151-1) demonstrated that glyoxylate reacts with hydroxylamine in the presence of Fe0/Fe2+ at 70°C to produce glycine. This is the same mechanism that converts pyruvate to alanine, applied to the C2 keto acid. Greigite (Fe3S4), a structural analog of biological [Fe4S4] clusters, provides additional catalytic iron-sulfur surfaces in vent settings.

reactions.name: Peracetic acid amination to glycine

conditions.other: Submarine hydrothermal system, elevated temperature and pressure

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic attack of ammonia on the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of peracetic acid, displacing the peroxide leaving group (-OOH) and forming the C-N bond of glycine. The peroxide acts as a good leaving group, making this an energetically favorable amination. Pyrite and magnetite surfaces may orient the reactants and lower the activation barrier.
reactions.reasoning: Aubrey, Cleaves, and Bada (2008, Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres, DOI: 10.1007/s11084-008-9153-2) demonstrated that peracetic acid reacts with ammonia to produce glycine under simulated submarine hydrothermal vent conditions. Pyrite (FeS2) and magnetite (Fe3O4) are abundant minerals at hydrothermal vents that provide catalytic surfaces for organic reactions.

reactions.name: L-Serine retro-aldol thermal decomposition to glycine and formaldehyde

conditions.other: Sustained hydrothermal temperatures; retro-aldol cleavage is kinetically favored

reactions.mechanism: Retro-aldol cleavage of the C-C bond between the alpha-carbon and the hydroxymethyl side chain of serine. The hydroxymethyl group is released as formaldehyde (CH2O), yielding glycine. This is the reverse of the aldol condensation that forms serine from glycine + formaldehyde. Under sustained hydrothermal conditions, the retro-aldol direction is favored.
reactions.reasoning: Bada, Shou, Man, and Schroeder (1978, Earth and Planetary Science Letters, DOI: 10.1016/0012-821X(78)90042-0) demonstrated that L-serine decomposes to glycine and formaldehyde via retro-aldol cleavage under thermal conditions. Mineral surfaces (pyrite, magnetite) at hydrothermal vents provide catalytic sites that can lower the activation energy for C-C bond cleavage.

reactions.name: L-Threonine thermal decomposition to glycine and ethylene oxide

conditions.other: High-temperature hydrothermal degradation; threonine is thermally unstable

reactions.mechanism: Retro-aldol-type cleavage of the C-C bond between the glycine moiety (C-alpha and carboxyl) and the threonine side chain (2-hydroxyethylamine fragment). The C2 side chain is released as ethylene oxide via intramolecular cyclization of the 2-aminoethanol fragment after deamination. Glycine is the major amino acid product.
reactions.reasoning: Bada, Shou, Man, and Schroeder (1978, Earth and Planetary Science Letters, DOI: 10.1016/0012-821X(78)90042-0) characterized the decomposition of L-threonine to glycine + ethylene oxide. Vallentyne (1964, Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta, DOI: 10.1016/0016-7037(64)90147-4) first demonstrated that L-threonine thermally degrades to glycine as the major product. Iron-sulfur minerals (pyrite, greigite) at vents provide catalytic surfaces.

reactions.name: L-Asparagine hydrolytic decomposition to glycine

conditions.other: Deep-sea hydrothermal vent temperatures; multi-step hydrolysis and degradation yielding two equivalents of glycine per asparagine

reactions.mechanism: Multi-step hydrolytic decomposition: (1) hydrolysis of the side-chain amide of asparagine to aspartate with release of NH3, (2) decarboxylation and/or retro-aldol cleavage of the C4 backbone to yield two glycine molecules. The net result is that one asparagine plus water produces two equivalents of glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Qian, Engel, Macko, Carpenter, and Deming (1993, Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta, DOI: 10.1016/0016-7037(93)90540-D) demonstrated that L-asparagine undergoes hydrolytic decomposition at high temperature (relevant to deep-sea hydrothermal vents) to produce two equivalents of glycine. This establishes glycine as a common degradation product of complex amino acids under hydrothermal conditions.

reactions.name: Formaldehyde Cannizzaro disproportionation to glycolic acid

conditions.other: Submarine hydrothermal system; two equivalents of formaldehyde undergo crossed Cannizzaro/aldol to yield one glycolic acid

reactions.mechanism: Cannizzaro-type disproportionation: one molecule of formaldehyde is oxidized to formic acid while the other is reduced. Subsequent aldol condensation of the resulting glycolaldehyde with formic acid yields glycolic acid. Alternatively, two formaldehyde molecules undergo crossed-Cannizzaro/aldol to produce the C2 alpha-hydroxy acid directly.
reactions.reasoning: Aubrey, Cleaves, and Bada (2008, Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres, DOI: 10.1007/s11084-008-9153-2) demonstrated that formaldehyde undergoes Cannizzaro-type disproportionation under submarine hydrothermal conditions to produce glycolic acid. This provides a route from the simplest aldehyde to a C2 intermediate intimately connected to the glyoxylate-glycine metabolic axis.

reactions.name: Glyoxylate reduction to glycolic acid

conditions.other: 70°C, aqueous, mild hydrothermal; reduction of aldehyde to alcohol

reactions.mechanism: Reduction of the aldehyde group of glyoxylate to a primary alcohol by Fe2+-mediated electron transfer, converting the alpha-keto acid to the alpha-hydroxy acid (glycolic acid). Under cyanosulfidic conditions (Ritson, 2021), sulfur species may also serve as reductants.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska, Varma, and Moran (2019, Nature, DOI: 10.1038/s41586-019-1151-1) demonstrated Fe2+-mediated reduction of glyoxylate to glycolic acid at 70°C. Ritson (2021, Science Advances, DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.abh3981) also showed this reduction under cyanosulfidic conditions. Glycolic acid is the alpha-hydroxy acid node of the C2 metabolic network, linked to the glyoxylate-glycine axis.

reactions.name: Acetate UV photoredox to glycolic acid

conditions.other: UV-irradiated shallow pool, 4 hours irradiation time, no ozone shielding

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven photoredox radical coupling: UV photons cleave the C-H bond of acetate to generate a carboxylate-stabilized carbon radical. This radical reacts with water-derived hydroxyl radical (or undergoes direct hydroxylation) to produce glycolic acid. TiO2 semiconductor photocatalysis enhances radical generation.
reactions.reasoning: Liu, Wu, Kufner, Sasselov, Fischer, and Sutherland (2021, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/s41557-021-00789-w) demonstrated UV-driven photoredox radical coupling of acetate with water over 4 hours to produce glycolic acid. This provides a photochemical route from the simplest carboxylate to the C2 alpha-hydroxy acid, feeding the broader glyoxylate-glycine metabolic network.

reactions.name: Glycine peptide bond formation (diglycine synthesis)

conditions.other: Wet-dry cycling in evaporitic pools; dehydration drives peptide bond condensation; two glycine molecules condense

reactions.mechanism: Condensation reaction: the amino group of one glycine molecule attacks the carboxyl carbon of a second glycine molecule, forming a peptide (amide) bond with loss of water. Montmorillonite clay adsorbs and concentrates glycine on its interlayer surfaces, orienting the molecules for peptide bond formation. Wet-dry cycling provides the thermodynamic driving force by removing water during the dry phase.
reactions.reasoning: Peptide bond formation from amino acids on clay surfaces under wet-dry cycling is a well-established prebiotic reaction (Lahav et al., 1978; Ferris et al., 1996). This represents the downstream biochemical utilization of glycine produced by hydrothermal and surface pathways, connecting the amino acid pool to proto-protein chemistry. Glycine, as the simplest and most abundant prebiotic amino acid, would be the primary substrate for early peptide formation.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of glycine and formaldehyde to serine

conditions.other: Aldol condensation; reverse of serine retro-aldol decomposition (rxn_007)

reactions.mechanism: Aldol condensation: the alpha-carbon of glycine is deprotonated under mildly alkaline conditions to form a carbanion (enolate equivalent), which attacks the carbonyl carbon of formaldehyde. The resulting adduct is serine (2-amino-3-hydroxypropanoic acid). This is the reverse of the retro-aldol cleavage of serine (rxn_007), creating a recycling loop between glycine and serine.
reactions.reasoning: Aubrey, Cleaves, and Bada (2008, Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres, DOI: 10.1007/s11084-008-9153-2) noted that serine decomposition to glycine + formaldehyde is the reverse of the aldol condensation. Under lower-temperature biochemical/surface conditions where retro-aldol is not favored, the forward aldol condensation can produce serine from glycine and formaldehyde. This reaction creates a glycine-serine recycling loop spanning hydrothermal (retro-aldol, rxn_007) and biochemical (aldol, rxn_014) environments.

pathways.name: Strecker-Cyanosulfidic Pathway (Surface)
pathways.description: Formaldehyde, HCN, and ammonia undergo Strecker synthesis to produce aminoacetonitrile in UV-irradiated cyanosulfidic surface pools. Aminoacetonitrile then hydrolyzes to glycine under aqueous conditions. This is the simplest case of the cyanosulfidic aminonitrile-to-amino acid pathway.

pathways.name: Iron-Metabolic Pathway via Isocitrate (Hydrothermal)
pathways.description: Isocitrate undergoes Fe2+-catalyzed retro-aldol cleavage at hydrothermal vents to produce glyoxylate and succinate. Glyoxylate is then reductively aminated by hydroxylamine with Fe0/Fe2+ catalysis to produce glycine. This represents the prebiotic analog of core metabolic reactions.

pathways.name: UV Photoredox to Reductive Amination (Surface → Hydrothermal)
pathways.description: 2,2-Dihydroxyacetic acid is dehydrated to glyoxylate by UV photoredox in surface pools. Glyoxylate is then transported to hydrothermal settings where it is reductively aminated to glycine by iron catalysis. This pathway demonstrates cross-environment interconnection.

pathways.name: Peracetic Acid Hydrothermal Amination
pathways.description: Peracetic acid reacts directly with ammonia under submarine hydrothermal conditions to produce glycine. This is a single-step route from an oxidized C2 precursor.

pathways.name: Serine Retro-Aldol Decomposition (Hydrothermal)
pathways.description: L-Serine undergoes retro-aldol thermal decomposition at hydrothermal temperatures to yield glycine and formaldehyde. This represents the thermal recycling of a C3 amino acid back to the simplest amino acid.

pathways.name: Threonine Thermal Decomposition (Hydrothermal)
pathways.description: L-Threonine thermally decomposes at hydrothermal temperatures to produce glycine and ethylene oxide. Glycine is the thermodynamically stable end-product of threonine degradation.

pathways.name: Asparagine Hydrolytic Decomposition (Hydrothermal)
pathways.description: L-Asparagine undergoes multi-step hydrolysis at deep-sea hydrothermal vent temperatures to produce two equivalents of glycine. This demonstrates glycine as a thermodynamic sink for amino acid degradation.

pathways.name: Formaldehyde Cannizzaro to Glycolate Network (Hydrothermal)
pathways.description: Two formaldehyde molecules undergo Cannizzaro disproportionation under hydrothermal conditions to produce glycolic acid, feeding the C2 metabolic network linked to the glyoxylate-glycine axis. Glycolic acid connects to glyoxylate via reversible redox chemistry.

pathways.name: Acetate UV Photoredox to Glycolate Network (Surface)
pathways.description: Acetate undergoes UV-driven photoredox radical coupling with water in surface pools to produce glycolic acid over 4 hours. This photochemical route feeds the C2 metabolic network from the simplest carboxylate.

pathways.name: Glycine-Serine Recycling Loop and Peptide Assembly (Hydrothermal ↔ Biochemical)
pathways.description: Glycine condenses with formaldehyde via aldol reaction under moderate biochemical conditions to produce serine (rxn_014). Serine can be thermally recycled back to glycine + formaldehyde at hydrothermal temperatures (rxn_007), creating a dynamic equilibrium. Accumulated glycine enters peptide bond formation on clay surfaces (rxn_013) to produce diglycine and higher peptides.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Classic Strecker pathway well-supported by Patel et al. (2015). Both steps operate in the surface environment with cyanosulfidic conditions. Formaldehyde + HCN + NH3 → aminoacetonitrile is textbook Strecker chemistry, and the subsequent nitrile hydrolysis to glycine is experimentally demonstrated. The pathway is self-consistent and directly aligned with the literature. Minor point: the surface environment could benefit from explicit mention of cyanosulfidic mineral/agent context (e.g., CuCN catalysis or H2S as reductant from the Patel framework), though the core chemistry is sound.
suggestions: Explicitly specify cyanosulfidic agents (e.g., H2S, Cu(I) cyanide) for the surface environment to strengthen mineral-agent compliance

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Directly mirrors the Muchowska et al. (2019) iron-catalyzed metabolic network. Both steps are hydrothermal with Fe⁰/Fe²⁺ catalysis, which is well-supported. The retro-aldol cleavage of isocitrate (rxn_003) and reductive amination of glyoxylate (rxn_005) are both experimentally demonstrated at 70°C. Main weakness: isocitrate (mol_013) has no upstream synthesis reaction in the network — it appears as an orphan intermediate. For a complete network, the source of isocitrate should be shown (e.g., citrate isomerization or a condensation route from oxaloacetate + acetyl-CoA analogs).
suggestions: Add an upstream reaction generating isocitrate from simpler precursors (e.g., citrate isomerization or aldol condensation of oxaloacetate and acetyl thioester)
Explicitly list Fe⁰ and Fe²⁺ as mineral catalysts in the reaction node metadata

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent cross-environment pathway: UV photoredox generation of glyoxylate at the surface (Liu et al., 2021) feeds into iron-catalyzed reductive amination in hydrothermal settings (Muchowska et al., 2019). This is the strongest demonstration of Surface → Hydrothermal environmental flow in the network and is well-supported by independent literature. Glyoxylic acid hydrate dehydration under UV is plausible, and the downstream reductive amination is robust. Minor issue: glyoxylic acid hydrate (mol_015) also lacks an upstream source in the network.
suggestions: Add an upstream reaction producing glyoxylic acid hydrate (e.g., from glycolaldehyde oxidation or CO2 reduction)
Specify UV wavelength range (200-300 nm) in surface reaction conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Single-step hydrothermal amination of peracetic acid by ammonia, supported by Aubrey et al. (2008). The reaction is experimentally demonstrated but the mechanism is less well-characterized than other routes. Peracetic acid (mol_009) has no upstream synthesis in the network, and its prebiotic formation from acetate under oxidizing conditions is itself an open question (acknowledged in the literature review). The stoichiometry also raises questions: the conversion of CH3CO-OOH + NH3 to H2N-CH2-COOH involves both C-N bond formation and loss of a carbon-equivalent that is not accounted for properly.
suggestions: Add an upstream reaction for peracetic acid formation from acetate
Clarify the mechanism and atom balance for peracetic acid → glycine conversion
Consider whether this pathway should be labeled as speculative

pathway_evaluations.feedback: L-Serine retro-aldol to glycine + formaldehyde is well-established (Bada et al., 1978). The reaction is thermodynamically favorable at hydrothermal temperatures. The pathway coherence is slightly reduced because serine's only source in the network is rxn_014 (glycine + formaldehyde → serine, biochemical), which creates a circular dependency — serine is made from glycine and then decomposes back to glycine. This is acknowledged as a recycling loop in P10, but as a standalone pathway it doesn't provide de novo glycine synthesis.
suggestions: Add an independent prebiotic synthesis of serine (e.g., from glycolaldehyde + HCN + NH3 via Strecker) to break the circular dependency
Clarify that this pathway represents recycling rather than de novo synthesis

pathway_evaluations.feedback: L-Threonine thermal decomposition to glycine is supported by both Vallentyne (1964) and Bada et al. (1978). The chemistry is sound. However, L-threonine (mol_011) is a complete orphan in the network with no upstream synthesis reaction, severely weakening pathway coherence. For a prebiotic network, the origin of threonine must be addressed. The ethylene oxide co-product is an interesting mechanistic detail from Bada et al.
suggestions: Add a prebiotic synthesis route for threonine or note it as a starting material from exogenous delivery (e.g., meteoritic amino acids)
Consider linking threonine synthesis to the iron-catalyzed metabolic network

pathway_evaluations.feedback: L-Asparagine hydrolytic decomposition to glycine is supported by Qian et al. (1993). Two issues: (1) The literature states asparagine + H2O produces TWO equivalents of glycine, but the network only shows one glycine product — this is a stoichiometric error. (2) L-asparagine (mol_012) has no upstream synthesis in the network. The pathway is chemically valid but incompletely represented.
suggestions: Fix stoichiometry: rxn_009 should produce 2 glycine molecules as per Qian et al. (1993)
Add upstream asparagine synthesis or justify its prebiotic availability
Include intermediate steps (asparagine → aspartate → glycine) for mechanistic clarity

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Formaldehyde Cannizzaro disproportionation to glycolic acid is a supporting side reaction (Aubrey et al., 2008) that does not produce glycine. Glycolic acid (mol_006) is a dead-end in the network with no further reaction leading to glycine. The reaction itself has a stoichiometry issue: it should require 2 formaldehyde molecules but only shows one. This pathway contributes to network richness but not to target achievement.
suggestions: Fix stoichiometry to show 2 CH2O → glycolic acid
Add a reaction converting glycolic acid to glyoxylate (oxidation) to close the loop toward glycine
Re-label as supporting/side pathway rather than a glycine-producing pathway

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Acetate UV photoredox to glycolic acid (Liu et al., 2021) is a valid surface chemistry reaction but does not produce glycine. Like P8, glycolic acid is a dead-end — there is no reaction in the network oxidizing glycolic acid back to glyoxylate for subsequent reductive amination to glycine. The pathway supports network breadth but not target synthesis.
suggestions: Add glycolic acid → glyoxylate oxidation reaction to connect this pathway to glycine production
Consider this as feeding into the glyoxylate hub via an oxidation step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Interesting recycling loop: glycine + formaldehyde → serine (biochemical/aldol condensation, rxn_014), serine → glycine + formaldehyde (hydrothermal retro-aldol, rxn_007), then glycine → diglycine (biochemical peptide bond formation, rxn_013). This pathway demonstrates a plausible Hydrothermal ↔ Biochemical cycling loop. However, it is inherently circular for glycine — it consumes glycine to make serine and regenerates glycine from serine. The net useful output is diglycine (rxn_013). The biochemical peptide bond formation step (rxn_013) needs more specification of condensation conditions (mineral surfaces, wet-dry cycling, etc.).
suggestions: Specify condensation conditions for peptide bond formation (e.g., mineral surface catalysis, wet-dry cycling, carbonyl sulfide activation)
Clarify that this is a recycling/amplification loop rather than a de novo synthesis pathway
Add energy source or activating agent for the peptide bond formation step

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-constructed and literature-grounded synthesis network for glycine that successfully integrates seven distinct synthesis routes across three prebiotic environments. The major strengths are: (1) Excellent literature alignment — all six experimentally demonstrated pathways from the curated review are faithfully represented, with correct citations to Patel et al. (2015), Muchowska et al. (2019), Aubrey et al. (2008), Bada et al. (1978), Vallentyne (1964), Qian et al. (1993), and Liu et al. (2021). (2) Strong hub molecule architecture — glyoxylate (degree 4), formaldehyde (degree 4), glycine (degree 8), and ammonia (degree 3) function as well-connected hubs. (3) Good environmental flow — especially P3 (Surface → Hydrothermal via UV glyoxylate → iron reductive amination). (4) The concept of glycine as a thermodynamic attractor is well-supported by the decomposition pathways (P5-P7). Key weaknesses: (1) Several intermediate molecules are orphans with no upstream synthesis (isocitrate, threonine, asparagine, peracetic acid, glyoxylic acid hydrate), reducing network completeness. (2) Glycolic acid is a dead-end — three reactions produce it but none convert it back toward glycine, missing the glycolic acid → glyoxylate oxidation link. (3) Minor stoichiometric errors in rxn_009 (should produce 2 glycine) and rxn_010 (requires 2 formaldehyde). (4) Some reactions lack explicit mineral catalyst specification in metadata. The three strongest pathways (P1, P2, P3) are individually excellent and well above threshold, giving the network solid core viability despite peripheral weaknesses.
